title: Platform Bed with Side Storage and 1x12 Divider Shelf
summary: A full-size platform bed built on a torsion-box-style 2x4 frame, with a divided storage tower attached down one side in place of a nightstand.
description:
The frame does double duty as both the bed's structure and the storage tower's mounting surface — the storage piece attaches to a dedicated support 2x4 added across the top of the inner frame, rather than hanging off the outer trim, so the weight of anything stored there loads into the bed's structural frame and not into a piece of finish trim.

The source is explicit about handling this as two separate assemblies: build and finish the platform and the storage tower separately, then bring both into the bedroom and join them there. A bed this size, fully assembled, won't fit through most doorways or up a stairwell — a lesson worth taking at face value rather than discovering partway up the stairs.

materials.name: Plywood, 1/2", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Platform base under the mattress; scrap material also works.

cutList.note: Measure and cut to fit — ripped plywood or 1x4s also work.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: Cut the frame first. From the 2x4s cut two inner frame ends at 64-1/2", five joists at 72", two centre supports at 14-1/4" and the storage support at 26" — be aware that those ten pieces alone consume all seven supplied 8 ft 2x4s, so the eleven 77" mattress slats and any centre legs need stock you have not bought yet; the cut list's own note allows ripped plywood or 1x4s for the slats, which is the cheaper answer. From the two 8 ft 1x12s cut the storage top and bottom at 75" and two storage ends at 20-1/2", one long and one short piece per board with almost nothing left over. From the 1x10 cut two dividers at 19" and three shelves at 24-1/2". From the 8 ft 1x6s cut three planked back boards at 75", and from the 1x3 one back filler at 75". Leave the outer frame trim — two 75" sides and two 66" ends from the 12 ft 1x6s — and the plywood platform base uncut until the frame is standing, since the cut list marks all of it measure-and-cut-to-fit.

steps.title: Build the inner frame
steps.body: Lay two of the 72" joists and both 64-1/2" ends out as a rectangle on a flat floor, with the 64-1/2" ends running the full width and the 72" joists between them. That gives a frame 64-1/2" wide by 75" long overall, which the trim confirms twice over: the side trim is 75" and the end trim is 66", which is 64-1/2" plus 3/4" of side trim at each end. Drive 3" self-tapping screws through the 64-1/2" ends into the joist ends, two per corner, predrilling near the ends so the 2x4 does not split. Measure both diagonals corner to corner and shift the frame until they match before anything else goes on.

steps.title: Add the remaining joists
steps.body: Add the three remaining 72" joists between the ends, spaced evenly across the 64-1/2" width. With five joists that works out to 15-3/4" on centre, leaving 14-1/4" of clear space between each pair — which is exactly the length of the centre supports, so use one of them as a spacer gauge and the spacing cannot go wrong. Screw through the 64-1/2" ends into each joist with 3" self-tapping screws, predrilling as before. Then fit the two 14-1/4" centre supports between joists at the middle of the span to stop them twisting under load.

steps.title: Add the outer side trim
steps.body: Measure the assembled frame rather than trusting the 75" on the drawing, and cut each side trim piece to your actual frame. Run glue along the outside face of the 2x4 frame and pin the 1x6 trim on with 1-1/4" brad nails, holding it so the top edge of the trim sits 1-1/2" below the top of the frame. That 1-1/2" reveal is deliberate — it is the recess the plywood platform base drops into, so it has to be consistent along the whole length. Lay a scrap of 2x4 flat on top of the frame and use its 1-1/2" thickness as a gauge, sliding it along as you nail.

steps.title: Add the outer end trim
steps.body: Cut the two end trim pieces to fit — 66" as drawn, which is the frame's 64-1/2" width plus the 3/4" of side trim now standing proud at each end. Glue and brad-nail them on with the same 1-1/2" reveal at the top. These are butt joints, not miters, so cut each end piece last and shave it back until it presses in with the corners tight; a gap here is at knee height and impossible to miss.

steps.title: Attach the storage support
steps.body: Lay the 26" 2x4 storage support flat across the top of the inner frame on whichever side the storage tower will stand, and screw it down into the joists with 3" self-tapping screws. This piece is why the tower does not need legs: everything stored in it loads through this support into the bed's structural frame rather than into a piece of 1x6 finish trim, which would eventually pull off the nails holding it. Position it where the tower's mid-length will fall. That completes the platform.

steps.title: Build the storage box
steps.body: The storage tower is a long shallow box, and it matters which way round you build it: the two 20-1/2" 1x12 end pieces run the full height and the 75" top and bottom sit between them, so the tower finishes 76-1/2" long and 20-1/2" tall with a 75" by 19" interior. The 19" dividers and the planked back — three 5-1/2" boards plus a 2-1/2" filler, 19" of width in total, all 75" long — both confirm that reading, and building it the other way round leaves every one of those parts the wrong size. Join the corners with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails, or 2" screws, and check the diagonals as you go; at 76-1/2" long this box will happily go together as a parallelogram if you let it.

steps.title: Add the dividers
steps.body: Stand the two 19" dividers inside the box, inset 3/4" from the front edge so their 9-1/4" width leaves a lip at the front of the 11-1/4"-deep box. Mark both the top and the bottom panel for each divider before you fasten anything, so the divider goes in plumb rather than leaning, and hold it against a square while you glue and nail through the top and bottom with 1-1/4" brads. The two dividers split the 75" interior into three bays — put them where you want the bays and mirror the two positions so the tower reads as deliberate rather than accidental.

steps.title: Add the shelves
steps.body: Cut two 9-1/4"-long blocks and use them inside the box as a spacing guide for the shelves. Attach the two outer shelves with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails. For the middle shelf, toenail from the front at an angle into the vertical dividers.

steps.title: Add the planked back
steps.body: Mark the location of every divider and shelf on the inside lip of the box. Dry-fit the back boards to check the fit, then remove, apply glue to all edges, and reattach one board at a time, nailing through the outer sides into each back board's side and top edges.

steps.title: Join the platform and storage box
steps.body: Finish the platform and the storage tower separately before they ever meet. A full bed with a 76-1/2" tower already attached will not go through a doorway or round a stairwell, and this is worth taking at face value rather than discovering halfway up the stairs. Bring both into the bedroom, stand the tower against the side of the bed, and drive 2" self-tapping screws up through the tower's bottom panel into the bed frame, plus more through the tower into the 26" storage support on the mattress side. That support is what carries the load, so make sure at least two screws land in it.

steps.title: Install feet and add center legs
steps.body: Install furniture feet or caster wheels per the manufacturer's instructions. If additional support is desired, add center legs cut from 2x4, spaced about a third of the way down, and attach with 3" self-tapping wood screws.

steps.title: Add the platform base and slats
steps.body: Cut the 1/2" plywood platform base to fit inside the 1-1/2" reveal you left at the top of the trim, holding it back on the storage side so the tower can sit tight against the frame. Screw it down into the joists with 2" screws and drill a few ventilation holes through it so the mattress can breathe. If you are adding slats over the base, cut them to your own frame and space them about 4" apart, fastening with the same 2" self-tapping screws — this plan lists only 3" and 2" screws, and 2" is the right length through a 1/2" base into a 2x4. Sand every exposed edge and corner before the bedding goes on.

images.alt: Platform bed with attached side storage tower and divider shelf
